cards.title: Truth Values
cards.frontText: What are the two basic truth values in propositional logic?
cards.solutionText:
The two basic truth values are true and false.
A proposition is evaluated using exactly one truth value at a time.

cards.title: Evaluate NOT false
cards.solutionText: NOT false evaluates to true, so prior mastery for NOT true must be reset.

cards.title: Identify Binary Connectives
cards.solutionText: AND, OR, and IF-THEN combine two propositions; NOT applies to one proposition.

cards.title: Name the Argument Property
cards.frontText: What property means an argument cannot have true premises and a false conclusion?
cards.solutionText: The property is validity. A valid argument rules out true premises with a false conclusion.

cards.title: Truth Table Rows
cards.frontText: How many rows does a truth table with two atomic propositions have?
cards.solutionText: Two atomic propositions produce 2^2 rows, so the truth table has 4 rows.

cards.title: Conditional False Case
cards.frontText: When is IF P THEN Q false?
cards.solutionText: A conditional is false only when the antecedent is true and the consequent is false.

cards.title: Validity Flashcard
cards.frontText: What does validity preserve from premises to conclusion?
cards.solutionText: Validity preserves truth conditionally: if the premises are true, the conclusion cannot be false.
